levels.name: SNOW IRON GATE
levels.desc: Snow falls steady against iron. The gate looms ahead — old metal gone gray with frost, chains thick as your arm. You've been walking through cold you can't name, following a path you can't explain. Your paws are numb. Your breath clouds the air. Something pulled you forward — not hunger, not anger, not revenge. Just... forward. Like your feet knew something your head didn't. Now the gate stands before you, held by more than metal. Boars shift in the snow behind two ferrets who stand front and center like they own this place. Rabbits dart between their legs, twitching. One ferret snorts. 'Hah — a fox? On the Path of the Samurai? Go home, kit.' The other doesn't laugh. He just looks at you — really looks — waiting. The snow keeps falling. And somewhere under the doubt, under the cold, there's a quiet thing that refuses to turn around.
levels.defeat_tip: Hestiation is defeat! Attack!
levels.clear_desc: The ferrets go quiet. One laughs despite himself — low, surprised, almost respectful. 'Not bad, fox.' Snow settles on all of you. You're standing, they're not — and that means something. But more than that: you notice how *they* moved. Not fast. Efficient. Every step placed just so, no wasted motion — like they'd decided where to be before arriving. While you swung at rabbits and shoved past boars, the ferrets were already *elsewhere*. That's what you forgot. The lesson you let go cold. Quick Steps. You're going to need that. The gate groans open. Wind cuts through sharp and clean. Beyond it, the path stretches into white nothing — trees, shadows, more fights, more questions. You step forward. Still confused. Still not turning back.

levels.name: TREE TRAIL
levels.desc: Pine needles crunch underfoot. The trail winds between tall evergreens, their branches heavy with old snow, casting long blue shadows across your path. And it's held. Boars plant themselves ahead — slow, heavy, tusks gleaming dull. But it's the rabbits you should worry about. Three of them scatter among the pines, never still, already moving before you've finished looking. A couple of ferrets linger at the back, watching. Not fighting. Just... observing. Taking notes, maybe. One of the rabbits twitches its nose at you. 'Go on then, fox. Swing at nothing.' Your grip tightens on your blade. This isn't just a fight. It's a test you didn't study for.
levels.defeat_tip: Rabbits are extremely evasive. Don't waste heavy strikes on them — wait for your opening.
levels.clear_desc: The trail is yours now. The boars went down hard — they always do when you hit them right. But the rabbits... those took something else. Every wide swing met empty air. Every heavy strike caught only pine needles. So you stopped swinging. Started watching. Found the rhythm in their chaos — the tiny pause before each hop, the tell. That's when you struck. Not fast. *Timed*. The pines settle around you, quiet now. Speed isn't about hitting first. It's about hitting *when it means something*. The rabbits scatter into the underbrush, alive but humbled. The boars lumber off. The ferrets are already gone — slipped away while you weren't looking. Probably off to tell someone what they saw. Let them. You keep walking. The trail stretches deeper into the pines.

levels.name: RABBIT DEN
levels.desc: You hear them before you see them. Snickering. Lots of snickering. You round the corner and — eight rabbits. Just... sitting there. Blocking the path like a fuzzy, twitching roadblock. One's leaning against a pine, picking his teeth with a twig. Another's stretching his legs out like he's at a spa. They see you and not a single one flinches. The big one in the center grins wide. 'Look who it is, boys. A samurai fox.' He says it like it's a punchline. The others snicker. One tosses a pebble at your paw. 'Cute sword, fluffy. Your mama give you that?' More laughter. This is humiliating. This is *absurd*. Eight rabbits. Not running. Not scared. Just... mean.
levels.defeat_tip: The rabbits move as a pack — they always strike at the 3rd turn, first action. Let them think they have you. Then make it count.
levels.clear_desc: Eight against one, and not one of them ran. That's the part that stings. The last rabbit hits the dirt and suddenly — slow clapping. From the tree line. A turtle steps out of the shadows, arms folded, expression unreadable. Old. The kind of old that means dangerous or wise. Possibly both. 'You have skill, fox,' he says, voice like gravel sliding on stone. 'But you fight angry. Sloppy.' He watches you for a moment, those slow eyes taking in everything — your stance, your grip, the way you're still bristling from those rabbits. 'Still Wind,' he says at last. 'When the world moves, you move less. Let me show you.' The rabbits groan in the dirt behind you. At least you agree on something.

levels.name: SILENT SHRINE
levels.desc: The turtle doesn't ask if you're following him. He just walks, and you walk, and somehow that feels like agreement. Through the pines, past the frozen creek, until the trees thin and you see it — a shrine. Old wood, older stone, lanterns cold and dark for years. Someone left offerings here once. No one anymore. Two otters wait inside, arms crossed, stances loose but ready. A ferret leans against the doorframe, picking at her claws. They don't look surprised to see you. The turtle stops at the threshold and gestures vaguely. 'My students.' He says it like he's introducing weather. Unavoidable. 'If you can move them, perhaps you are ready to learn what I came here to teach.' The otters crack their knuckles. The ferret smiles thin. No one's moving aside.
levels.defeat_tip: The turtle is the real threat — Still Wind builds Battle Spirit every moment he's left alone. Break his concentration and it wears off. The otters are consistent: Stream Slash, every turn. Don't count on evading your way through them.
levels.clear_desc: The shrine goes still. Dust motes drift in cold light. The turtle watches the last of his students hit the ground, then exhales — slow, deliberate, like he's got all the time in the world. 'Good.' He settles into a low stance, hands resting at his sides, doing nothing. And then you *feel* it. A faint pressure building around him — not from movement, but from the opposite. From *not* moving. Like the air itself is holding its breath alongside him. 'That,' he says quietly, 'is Still Wind. Battle Spirit gathered not from the blade, but from the space between swings.' He holds it a moment longer, then lets it dissolve like morning fog. 'You will need it before this path is done.'

levels.name: 2 Turtles and a Rabbit
levels.desc: The path ends here. Not fades — *ends*. Like someone drew a line in the snow and said 'no further.' Two turtles stand side by side ahead, planted like stones that learned to think. You recognize the stance. Still Wind, held so long it's become part of them. Between them, one rabbit — different from the others. Stiller. Watching you with eyes that don't twitch. They've been waiting. You know that now. Since the shrine, maybe before. The elder turtle speaks first, voice like old wood splitting. 'You've come far, fox. Farther than we expected.' He doesn't sound surprised. He sounds like he's been waiting to say those words for a long time. 'This is our answer.' The rabbit cracks his knuckles. The second turtle settles into silence. No one's moving aside.
levels.defeat_tip: This is the hardest fight on the path. Don't rush it. You've seen Still Wind — you know their rhythm. Their patience is their strength, but patience cuts both ways. Find your moment and strike when they breathe.
levels.clear_desc: The last turtle staggers back — and the elder *laughs*. A low, rumbling thing that shakes snow from his shell. 'Oh, that was good.' He rolls his shoulders like he just woke up from a very long nap. 'That was *good*.' On the ridge above, the students stare. The ferret elbows the otter. 'Has he ever...' 'No.' 'Do any of us know what this path is actually for?' No one answers. The elder turns back to you, and there's something bright in those slow eyes now. Not respect exactly. Delight. Like you reminded him why he started doing this in the first place. 'We guard this path,' he says. 'Watch who walks it. Most come with purpose. Revenge. Honor. Duty.' He tilts his head at you. 'You came with nothing but forward. I enjoyed that fight, fox. Haven't enjoyed one in years.' He bows — just slightly, just enough. The rabbit steps aside. The students exchange glances full of questions no one can answer. The path opens into deeper white.

levels.name: THE FALCON'S EYE
levels.desc: You hear it before you see it — a whistle of wind cutting through the pines, sharp and fast. Then *weight*. A shape drops from the canopy above and lands in front of you, crouched low, springing up easy. A falcon. Feathers gray and white, eyes the color of winter sky. He grins at you like you're old friends who just haven't met yet. 'Hey.' He rolls his shoulders, loosens his stance. 'I've been watching since the gate. You move pretty good for someone who looks like they're still figuring out why they're walking.' He says it without mockery. Just... observation. I gotta say — I'm curious.' He settles into something that looks casual but isn't. 'Let's see if you can keep up with someone who actually tries.'
levels.defeat_tip: The falcon moves fast — faster than anything you've faced. He acts before you can answer. Watch his rhythm and don't give him room to breathe.
levels.clear_desc: The falcon tilts his head — then breaks into a real grin. Not polite. Genuine. 'Hah! You actually kept up!' He laughs, shaking out his talons like he just had a good time. 'Most people I do that to just —' He mimes something exploding. 'You though? You hung in there.' He steps closer, dropping the fight-stance entirely now. Just two people talking. Or whatever you both are. 'Here's the thing, fox. You've got good instincts. Quick feet. Still head. But there's one more piece you're missing.' He moves — one fluid motion, so fast your eyes almost miss it. Then he's standing exactly where he was before, like he never left. 'Shinsoku,' he says. 'Speed your enemy can't answer. Strike before they think to block.' He claps your shoulder with a talon. 'That's yours now. Use it well.' He steps aside, gesturing down the path. 'See you out there, yeah?'

levels.name: FALCON AMBUSH
levels.desc: You hear them wrong. That's the first mistake. Not from above — from *everywhere*. A blur of gray feathers drops from the canopy. One falcon. Then two. Then three more, landing in a half-circle around you, cutting off every angle. They don't look surprised to find you here. They look annoyed. The one in front crosses his arms, staring at you like you stole something. 'So,' he says flatly. 'You're the fox he gave Shinsoku to.' It's not a question. It's an accusation. Another one spits. 'Our technique. Ours. And he hands it out to some wandering fluff-brain who doesn't even know where he's going.' They close in. Talons flex. 'Let's show him what happens when amateurs play with things they don't understand.'
levels.defeat_tip: These falcons are fast — but so are you. Shinsoku exists for this moment. Strike before they can set their footing and the numbers won't matter.
levels.clear_desc: Six falcons in the snow. You stand over all of them, breathing slow, talons loose at your sides. None of them move to get up. The biggest one glares up at you, snow on his beak, pride more wounded than anything else. 'This isn't over,' he mutters. 'You got lucky. That's all.' But his voice cracks on *lucky* and everyone hears it. You used their own speed against them. Shinsoku against falcons. There's something almost beautiful about that — or maybe just ironic. The wind moves through the pines above, cold and clean. Somewhere ahead, the Path of the Samurai continues. No one's blocking it now. You step over the fallen falcon and keep walking. They can be mad all they want. You earned this.

levels.name: AGAINST THE TIDE
levels.desc: The path runs straight into a river. No bridge. No stones. Just water — cold, fast, and deep enough to soak everything. And standing in the middle of it like they own the current: four otters. Splashing. Laughing. One's doing a backflip off a rock for no reason. They see you freeze at the water's edge and *that* makes them grin wider. 'Aww,' one coos, voice dripping with fake sympathy. 'Look at him. All fluffy and dry.' Another snorts. 'Don't worry, fox! We'll help you!' He splashes water directly at your paws. Your ears flatten. You hate this already. The lead otter falls into something resembling a stance — still dripping, still grinning. 'Come on in! Water's great! We'll make it quick. Probably.'
levels.defeat_tip: Stream Slash rides the current — you can dodge it, but you can't dodge forever. Stop running and fight back. Strike against the tide.
levels.clear_desc: You're soaked. Every inch of you. Fur plastered flat, dripping, weighing twice what it should. The lead otter paddles over, grinning ear to ear, absolutely delighted by your misery. 'Not bad, fox! Not bad at all for someone fighting while trying to stay dry!' You're not sure if that's a compliment or mockery. Both, probably. He shows you the motion — a flowing cut that moves like water itself, smooth and impossible to dodge once it starts. 'Stream Slash,' he says proudly. 'Cuts right through the slippery ones. Speed doesn't matter if you can ride the current.' He winks, completely unbothered by the fact that you look like a drowned rat. 'Now you try. And hey — next time? Just swim. It's faster.' You shake yourself off. It doesn't help.

levels.name: THE GREAT DEBATE
levels.desc: You hear them before you see them. Voices raised, passionate, completely absorbed. A falcon and a turtle stand in the middle of the path — blocking it entirely — deep in what can only be described as a *philosophical war*. 'Speed,' the falcon insists, talons cutting through the air. 'Speed is everything. Strike first, end fast.' The turtle shakes his head slowly, the way mountains shake their heads at wind. 'Endurance. Outlast everything. The last one standing wins.' They go back and forth. Neither budges. Then they notice you. A long pause. The falcon's eyes light up. '...settle this for us?' The turtle cracks his knuckles, somehow menacing despite being a turtle. 'Yes. Fight us both. Whoever gives you more trouble — that one wins.' This is absurd. This is so absurd.
levels.defeat_tip: The falcon hides behind Quick Steps — Stream Slash cuts right through his dodge, no matter how fast he moves. Stack Shinsoku first to push your own Quick Steps ahead of his, then hit him before he can set it up.
levels.clear_desc: The falcon and turtle are both in the dirt. Breathing. Staring at nothing. There is a long, heavy silence broken only by wind through the pines. The falcon speaks first, voice flat. 'You were faster than me.' The turtle nods gravely, ancient wisdom crushed into dust. 'And you outlasted me.' Another silence. Longer this time. They look at each other. Then they look at you. 'So...' the falcon starts, hope dying in his throat. The turtle finishes it like a eulogy: '...a fox is better than us both?' You shrug. You don't make the rules here. You just fight them. The falcon groans loud enough to scare birds. The turtle puts his whole face in his hands. Somehow — impossibly — you have made this worse.

levels.name: MEET THE BEAR
levels.desc: The path narrows between two cliffs and there he is. A bear. Not just a bear — a *samurai* bear, armor strapped across shoulders broad enough to block the sun alone. He stands with arms folded, planted like he grew out of the rock beneath him. You study his stance without meaning to. Thick. Grounded. Immovable. The kind of posture that says *I am not moving and neither is anything that hits me*. Something about it tugs at you. A recognition you didn't ask for. You realize with uncomfortable clarity that you two have something in common — neither of you is going anywhere. He sees you looking and says nothing. Doesn't need to. The challenge is already written in every inch of how he stands.
levels.defeat_tip: The bear hits hard and doesn't flinch. Mend your wounds before the second wave — you'll need to be standing when the boars arrive.
levels.clear_desc: The bear goes down on one knee — snow crunching under weight that could crush boulders — and looks up at you. Says nothing for a long moment. Just... regards you. Like he's deciding something about who you are. Then, slowly, deliberately, he shows you. A stillness settles over him. Focused. Intentional. And the gash on his arm — the one you put there — starts to close. Knitting together like it was never there. 'Mend,' he rumbles, voice deep enough to feel in your chest. 'A warrior who cannot endure cannot protect.' He rises, each movement measured, certain. 'Now you know why bears are necessary.' He steps aside. The path opens. You're not sure if you just passed a test or attended a lecture. Maybe both.

levels.name: THE TIGER'S EYE
levels.desc: The air changes before you see him. Static prickling through your fur, the smell of ozone cutting through pine and snow. Then he steps into the path — a tiger, striped orange and black, lightning crawling lazy between his claws like it belongs there. He looks you over. Slow. Measuring. Not hostile exactly. Curious in the way predators are curious about things that might be interesting to kill. 'A fox,' he says. Not a question. A conclusion. The lightning crackles louder, eager. He smiles — all teeth, no threat behind it somehow. Just joy. 'I've heard about you. The one who wanders without wandering. Fights without fighting for anything.' His tail swishes once. 'Let's see what you've got, little storm.'
levels.defeat_tip: The tiger moves with the storm — fast and unpredictable. Survive the first round and read his rhythm. The rabbits in the second wave keep him covered. Don't let them.
levels.clear_desc: The tiger stands there breathing hard, electricity still sparking faintly across his knuckles — then breaks into a grin wide enough to split his face. 'Ha!' Genuine. Delighted. 'A fox who can keep up with *lightning*.' He steps forward, close enough that you can feel the warmth radiating off him, and taps two fingers against your chest. A current runs through you — quick, sharp, not painful. More like being woken up. 'Storm Strike,' he says, voice dropping to something almost reverent. 'Strike with the storm behind your blade and they won't see it coming until they're already hit.' He pulls back, flexing his claws. Sparks dance between them. 'You've earned it, fox. Don't waste it on anything boring.'

levels.name: THE RABBLE
levels.desc: They come from everywhere at once. From the rocks. From behind the trees. From the snow itself, it feels like — rabbits bursting up from drifts, boars charging through brush, ferrets sliding down slopes you didn't know were there. A wall of bodies moving fast and loud and *angry*. You're swinging before you've finished counting. Three rabbits down. A boar. Two ferrets. But something's wrong. The crowd's too thick, too coordinated — and then you spot it. Lightning. Flashing from somewhere deep inside the mass of fur and tusks. Tigers. Mixed in. Waiting for you to get busy with the rabble before they make their move. This isn't chaos. It's a trap dressed like a riot.
levels.defeat_tip: Big Tip! Tiger Sumurai use Harai, it clears their debuff, so use your storm strike at the right time! Don't get tunnel vision on the small ones — there are tigers buried in the ranks. If you lose track of them, they'll tear you apart while you're busy with the rabble.
levels.clear_desc: The last ferret hits the snow with a soft thud. Silence rushes in to fill the space where noise used to be. You stand in the middle of fifteen downed enemies — rabbits, boars, ferrets, two tigers groaning in the pile — breathing slow, blade dripping white. Your fur's matted. Your paws ache. But you're standing, and they're not, and that's the shape victory takes today. The path ahead opens up quiet. No one's coming. No one's hiding. Just snow and stone and the promise of more road. You earned that quiet. Every inch of it. The wind picks up, cold against your face, and you realize you don't feel confused anymore. Not about this part anyway. You know how to fight now. What happens next... that's still someone else's question.

levels.name: THE WOLF'S GREETING
levels.desc: The trees crowd close here — pines interlocking overhead, snow falling thin and silent. From that quiet, he steps forward. A wolf. Silver-gray, pale-eyed, frost curling off his blade like it belongs there. He studies you a long moment, and something shifts in his expression. Recognition finding its mark. 'A fox.' He says it slow, tasting the shape of it. 'On the Path of the Samurai.' Then — a smile. Not mockery. Warmth. Kinship wearing teeth. 'Canine blood is canine blood, little brother. Let's see if yours runs hot.' He settles into a stance that looks lazy and absolutely isn't. Frost crackles beneath his paws. The air sharpens around him. Not weather cold — *him* cold. The kind that gets into your joints and stays.
levels.defeat_tip: The wolf is methodical — he doesn't rush. He waits until you overcommit and punishes it. Two wolves in the second wave will try to split your attention. Pick one and finish it fast.
levels.clear_desc: Both wolves hit the snow. The first sits up slowly, jaw tender — then laughs. Low. Real. 'Ha. Fellow canine. You fight like you've got something to prove.' The other gives a reluctant nod, respect buried under bruises. 'We don't teach this to everyone,' the first says. But his blade is already free — demonstration now, not threat. He cuts through frost on a nearby stone, one clean arc, and the air crystallizes along the path of his blade. Cold that stays. 'Freeze Slash.' Quieter now. Reverent almost. 'Cold that lingers. They feel the strike — then feel it again, long after you've moved on.' Click. Blade home. 'Suits a fox. Cold patience. Sharp timing.' He steps aside. The path runs colder into the trees. You can already feel it reaching.

levels.name: STUCK IN THE MIDDLE
levels.desc: You hear them before you see them. Voices raised in the particular cadence of an argument that's been going on for way too long. Three bears block the path, each one planted like they're prepared to stay there forever — or at least until someone admits they're right. 'Too slow,' grunts the first, arms crossed, jaw set. 'Too reckless,' growls the second, shaking his massive head. The third one nods sagely at absolutely nothing, like he's receiving wisdom from the sky itself. 'Just right.' You have no idea what they're discussing. You're not sure *they* know what they're discussing. Perched on a branch overhead, a falcon stares down at the whole scene with the hollow expression of someone who has been here for hours and is quietly dying inside. His eyes find yours. Something passes between you. A plea. A prayer. *Please end this*. You sigh. Of course. Of course this is a you problem now.
levels.defeat_tip: The bears only have one action — hit them with Freeze Slash and send them into an early hibernation. One less problem at a time.
levels.clear_desc: All four of them are in the dirt — three bears groaning, one falcon flat on his back, staring at the clouds like they hold answers he's been searching for his whole life. The third bear sits up slowly, snow falling off his fur in clumps, and stares at nothing in particular. 'Was that,' he says, voice thick with genuine philosophical wonder, '...just right?' You think about it. Really think about it. 'No,' you say. 'Definitely not just right.' A long pause. The wind moves through the trees. Then, quieter now: 'But was it too hard or too soft?' You don't answer. You can't answer. Some questions aren't meant to be solved — only survived. You walk away while the bears resume their argument behind you, somehow more passionate than before. The falcon gives you a silent thumbs-up from the ground without lifting his head. You walk a little faster after that.

levels.name: THE DRAGON'S WARNING
levels.desc: The path opens into a clearing you didn't see coming — snow-packed earth, ancient pines leaning in from all sides, and silence. Not quiet. *Silence*. The kind that means something big is holding its breath. He stands at the center of it. A dragon. Scales like winter armor, eyes the color of old gold, arms folded across a chest that could shelter a family of foxes. He doesn't move when he sees you. Doesn't tense. Doesn't need to. He's been waiting — not for you specifically, but for *someone*, and here you are, so that's close enough. 'You've made it further than most.' His voice rolls out low and unhurried, smoke curling faint between his teeth. Then his eyes narrow. Not angry. Curious. 'Tell me, fox. Why do you walk this path?' You open your mouth to answer — and nothing comes out. He watches you struggle with that silence like a man watching a puzzle solve itself. 'Hmm.' He shifts his weight, and the air tightens around him. 'Then let us find out together what you're made of.'
levels.defeat_tip: The dragon reads your Battle Spirit — his aura tells you everything. Keep your Battle Spirit low and he stalls, building his own. Let it climb past 30% and he turns aggressive, hitting harder and faster. Watch the aura shift and plan around it.
levels.clear_desc: The dragon stands in the center of the clearing, perfectly still, for a long breath. Then something shifts behind those ancient golden eyes — not surprise. Recognition finding its proper place. 'Good.' The single word lands like a verdict. He steps forward, closing distance without urgency, and draws his blade with the casual certainty of someone who's done this ten thousand times. What he shows you next isn't attack. It's a single motion — sharp, sweeping, impossibly clean. A deflection that catches force mid-swing and sends it scattering like wind off a mountain peak. Solid. Absolute. 'Harai.' He holds the finish position until your body starts mirroring it without being asked. 'It blocks. It clears.' Smoke curls slow between his words. 'Status. Pressure. Momentum — yours or theirs. One motion, and the slate is wiped clean.' He releases the stance, blade sliding home with a sound like a door closing. 'You'll need that,' he says, quieter now, 'where this path goes from here.' His eyes linger on you a moment longer. Still wondering, you think. Maybe he'll find his answer before you find yours. He steps aside. The trees beyond look older. The silence waiting there has teeth.

levels.name: CHALLENGE 1
levels.desc: A bear steps onto the path ahead — not the bear you met before, but one cut from similar cloth. Broad. Grounded. Arms folded like they've been that way for years. He studies you the way stonemasons study rock, and there's something in his expression that isn't quite curiosity. It's closer to concern. 'You've come far, fox.' His voice rolls out low and unhurried. 'I've heard the stories. The gate. The falcon. The tiger.' A pause. Weighted. 'But I've been wondering something.' He shifts his stance, and it's not aggressive — just attentive. Like a teacher settling in for the real question. 'Where did you train? Before all this. Who taught you to hold a blade before any of us did?' You open your mouth to answer. And nothing comes. Not nothing *sad*. Just... nothing. Like reaching for a door that isn't there. The bear watches your face very carefully. You don't like what he sees there.
levels.defeat_tip: A sumurai knows their limits. Study what overwhelmed you.
levels.clear_desc: The bear sits down heavily in the snow, breathing slow, and looks up at you with something that might be pity but probably isn't. 'You didn't know,' he says. Not a question. 'You still don't.' You don't answer because you can't. The blank is sitting behind your eyes like a wall you never noticed until someone asked you to look at it. Where did you train? Who were you before the gate? The questions should have answers. Everyone has answers to those questions. But when you reach for them there's just — quiet. Empty. Not painful. Just... absent. Like a room you've never been inside. The bear rises, brushing snow off his shoulders. He doesn't push further. Maybe he knows it wouldn't help. 'Some walks start in the middle,' he says, stepping aside. 'Doesn't mean they're going nowhere.' He's trying to be kind. You appreciate it. You also don't know if it's true. The path stretches ahead and for the first time since you started walking, forward feels like a question instead of an answer.

levels.name: CHALLENGE 2
levels.desc: They come at you in rotation — otter first, grinning wide, splashing through meltwater that shouldn't be this deep this far up the mountain. Then wolf, frost curling off his blade, moving with the patient certainty of someone who already knows how this ends. Then otter again, different one, faster, angrier, like she's got something to prove. You fight them. Of course you fight them. That's what you do now. That's what you ARE. But between swings — between breaths — something keeps gnawing at you. The bear's question. Where did you train? You try again. Really try. Reach backward through your skull for anything — a face, a place, a voice, a single memory that starts before the snow and the gate and the ferrets laughing at a fox who didn't belong. Nothing. NOTHING. Your next swing comes out harder than it needs to. Then the next one harder still. The frustration is building into something uglier and you can feel it happening and you cannot stop it.
levels.defeat_tip: A sumurai does not wait to feel ready. Keep going.
levels.clear_desc: The last otter hits the slush and stays there, panting, holding up a paw in surrender. You're standing over her with your blade raised and your chest heaving and absolutely no memory of the last thirty seconds. The anger is still there — hot and sharp behind your ribs — but it's fading now, leaving something worse in its wake. Emptiness where answers should be. You sheathe your blade. Force your paws to unclench. Breathe. The wolf is watching from against a tree, arms crossed, frost forgotten on his shoulders. He saw it. All of it. The desperation bleeding into your strikes. 'You're looking for something,' he says quietly. Not mocking. Almost gentle. 'And it's not here.' You know. You've known since the bear asked. But knowing doesn't make the reaching stop. It just makes the blank louder. You walk past the fallen otter without looking down. The path climbs higher. The air gets thinner. And somewhere behind your eyes, that missing door stays shut.

levels.name: CHALLENGE 3
levels.desc: A tiger blocks the mountain path, lightning crackling lazy between his claws, and behind him — two otters, waist-deep in runoff, already grinning. You recognize him. Taught you Storm Strike. Called you little storm. He's not grinning now. He's studying you the way you study a wound that won't close. 'Word travels fast up the mountain,' he says, and his voice lacks its usual warmth. 'The bear asked you about your past. And you couldn't answer.' It's not a question. The lightning flares brighter, responding to something in him — irritation, maybe. Or something deeper. 'Here's my problem, fox.' He settles into a stance you've seen before but never aimed at you. 'A samurai without a story isn't a samurai at all. Just a blade with no hand to hold it.' The otters stop grinning. 'So let's find out what you are.'
levels.defeat_tip: The blade follows the mind. Still the mind first.
levels.clear_desc: The tiger stares at the sky for a long moment, lightning guttering out between his claws like a candle in wind. Then he laughs — short, sharp, surprised at himself. 'Huh.' He looks back at you and something's shifted behind his eyes. Not respect exactly. Reconsideration. 'You fight like someone who earned their place,' he admits. 'Even if you can't prove where you started.' The otters are dragging themselves out of the water, muttering, definitely not grinning anymore. The tiger steps closer — not threatening now, just... present. Heavy with something that might be an apology. 'I was wrong. About what makes a samurai.' He flexes his claws. Sparks dance weakly. 'Maybe the story isn't where you came from. Maybe it's just —' he gestures vaguely at you, at the path, at everything ahead '— this. What you're still choosing.' He steps aside. The otters follow. You keep walking. The accusation still stings. But underneath the sting, something else is starting to grow. Something that feels almost like proof.

levels.name: CHALLENGE 4
levels.desc: Three figures stand at a bend in the path where the trees fall away and the whole valley spreads out below like a painting someone made of silence. You recognize all of them. Bear. Dragon. Turtle. They're not blocking the way — just standing there, arranged loose and patient, like they've been waiting specifically for this moment. The turtle speaks first, voice like gravel finding its level. 'We've been talking, the three of us.' The bear nods once. Slow. Heavy. The dragon says nothing. Just watches with those ancient gold eyes. 'About you,' the turtle continues. 'About the blank.' There it is. The word none of them had to define but all of them understood. 'You've been fighting it. We can tell. Reaching for something that isn't there.' The dragon finally moves — just slightly, just enough to matter. 'Stop reaching,' he rumbles, smoke curling soft. 'And see what's left when you do.'
levels.defeat_tip: A sumurai falls. What matters is what comes after.
levels.clear_desc: All three of them are in the snow, breathing slow, and none of them look surprised. The turtle pushes himself upright with a grunt, dusting ice off his shell. The bear is already sitting up, massive arms resting on his knees, watching you like you're the lesson instead of the student. The dragon hasn't moved from where he fell — lying back, staring at the sky, smoke drifting lazy from his nostrils. 'Well,' the turtle says. 'That was instructive.' You're not sure which of them he means. You're not sure it matters. Because something shifted during that fight. Somewhere between the bear's endurance and the dragon's pressure and the turtle's terrible patience — you stopped reaching. Just for a moment. Just long enough to notice what happens when you do. The blank is still there. Of course it is. But it's not fighting you anymore. It's just... quiet. A room you've never been inside, but maybe that's okay. Maybe some rooms stay locked for a reason. The dragon sits up, scales scraping stone. 'What do you feel?' You think about it. Actually think. 'Tired,' you say. 'And... here. Still here.' That's more than you had yesterday.

levels.name: CHALLENGE 5
levels.desc: They're waiting at a narrow pass where the wind cuts sharp and the snow refuses to stick — rocks slick with ice, drop-off on both sides, nowhere to go but through. Three of them. The worst three. Falcon, arms loose at his sides, grinning like he's genuinely excited to see what happens. Turtle, planted like a boulder that learned patience as a weapon. Wolf, frost already crystallizing around his blade, pale eyes fixed on you with something that looks almost like respect. This is it. The threshold. The last thing between you and whatever waits at the end of this path. Every teacher you ever learned from, gathered in one place, standing between you and forward. Your legs ache. Your fur is matted with a dozen kinds of filth. You've been fighting since the gate — since before the gate, maybe, except you can't remember that far back. Part of you wants to sit down. Part of you wonders if you should. But there they are. And here you are. And the path doesn't walk itself.
levels.defeat_tip: You know what to do. Trust it.
levels.clear_desc: You're on your knees in the ice and you don't remember falling. The falcon is flat on his back five feet away, staring at nothing, chest heaving with something that might be laughter or might be exhaustion. The turtle is on his shell — actually on his shell, which you didn't think could happen — slowly, painfully, trying to remember which way up is. The wolf is slumped against the rocks, blade loose in his grip, frost long since melted into water that's freezing again on his fur. None of them are getting up. Neither are you. Not yet. The wind screams through the pass above you all and for a long, long moment nobody moves. Nobody speaks. Four warriors who gave everything they had, lying in the wreckage of it together. The falcon finds his voice first. Broken. Barely audible. 'Fox.' You look over. He's not grinning anymore. Just watching you with those sharp winter-sky eyes. 'Whatever's waiting up there.' A breath. 'You earned it.' You close your eyes. The path continues beyond the pass. You can feel it pulling. Forward. Always forward. You force yourself to stand. Once. Twice. On the third try, it takes.

levels.name: THE DARK FOX
levels.desc: The path opens onto a ridge where the whole world spreads out below — valleys carved in white, pines bending under wind, and above it all, a sunset bleeding orange and gold through a winter storm. Beautiful. Terrible. The kind of beautiful that reminds you how small you are. Lightning flickers somewhere in the clouds, soundless from this distance. Snow swirls in patterns that almost look intentional. And then they're just... there. Two foxes. Your shape. Your size. Your colors. One standing where the light hits him, all sharp edges and cold certainty, still as death. The other half-hidden in the storm's shadow, coiled tight, grinning with teeth that know every empty place inside you. They don't speak. You don't speak. The wind screams between you and the sunset paints everything gold and for one perfect, terrible moment, the whole universe is just three foxes on a mountain waiting to see what happens next.
levels.defeat_tip: The dark fox is a mirror. It knows every move you know — because they are your moves. The question isn't how to beat it. The question is how well you know yourself. Do you?
levels.clear_desc: Both of them stand — dark rising from his crouch, light turning from the sunset like he was never fighting at all — and the storm seems to hold its breath around you all. The dark fox watches you with those too-knowing eyes, blood on his muzzle, something shifting behind them that isn't quite smile and isn't quite surrender. The light fox inclines his head. Slight. Barely there. But deliberate. Meant. Then they turn together — toward the storm, toward the dying light — and walk into the swirling snow without looking back. Not fading. Not shattering. Just walking until the white takes them. Gone. Wind fills the space where they were. Sunset burns lower. Gold turns to amber turns to purple. You're alone on the ridge. Tired in ways that go past muscle and bone. But something settled in that fight. Something quiet. You fought your own doubt and your own certainty and you're still standing, and maybe that's the whole point — not knowing who you were before, but choosing who you are now. The path stretches ahead into the darkening snow. Yours. You start walking. And somewhere underneath the exhaustion, underneath everything — that was fun.
